title: Topic 155: Why Players Form Cliques: In-Group/Out-Group Dynamics in Gaming
content:
_**Topic 155: Why Players Form Cliques: In-Group/Out-Group Dynamics in Gaming**_

**ABSTRACT**

In the sprawling digital landscapes of online gaming, where heroes are forged and dragons are slain, a far more ancient and formidable beast lurks: human nature. This paper hilariously investigates the deeply ingrained tribalistic tendencies of gamers, who, despite having access to a global community, invariably resort to forming exclusive cliques. We delve into the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, applying the venerable tenets of Social Identity Theory to the chaotic world of Massively Multiplayer Online Role-Playing Games (MMORPGs). Through a series of real-world examples from gaming folklore, including the corporate espionage of *EVE Online* and the legendary guild drama of *World of Warcraft*, we expose the inherent absurdity of our digital social lives. Ultimately, this paper serves as a witty, academically-adjacent exploration of why we spend billions on technology to connect with everyone, only to immediately build walls and decide who is not cool enough to be on our raid team. It’s a digital high school cafeteria, and you’re not sitting with us.

**INTRODUCTION**

There is a universal, almost ritualistic, experience that binds all who have ever ventured into a new online game. It is the feeling of being the digital new kid, standing awkwardly in the virtual schoolyard of the starting zone, clad in the online equivalent of off-brand sneakers—the drab, statistically insignificant default armor. All around, impossibly cool players with glowing pauldrons and exotic mounts zip past, chattering in a guild channel that appears as an enigmatic, locked stream of text. You are an outsider, a “noob,” a digital plebeian peering through the gates of the popular kids’ clique. The central question that arises from this digital social anxiety is a profound one: in a world designed for mass participation, why do we so instinctively, and often ruthlessly, divide ourselves into “us” and “them”?

This paper posits that the formation of cliques in online gaming is not a flaw in the system but a fundamental feature of human psychology, hardwired into our collective consciousness long before the first pixel was ever rendered. We are, at our core, tribal creatures, and the digital realm is merely a new savanna on which to enact our ancient social dramas. This research will explore the psychological drivers behind this behavior, leaning heavily on the robust framework of Social Identity Theory, all while maintaining the satirical and humorous tone of a stand-up comedian with a PhD in sociology. We will dissect the seemingly irrational loyalty to one’s guild, the fierce rivalries between factions, and the petty dramas that can tear virtual worlds apart. By examining the in-group and out-group dynamics that define the social fabric of online gaming, we can gain a deeper, and funnier, understanding of not just our digital lives, but our consumer behavior and our very nature as social animals. So, grab your enchanted sword and your most cynical monocle as we embark on a quest to understand why, even in our wildest fantasies, we can’t help but be cliquey.

**LITERATURE REVIEW**

Before we dive headfirst into the glorious mess of gaming cliques, it is academically prudent to acknowledge the scholars who have bravely ventured into this digital wilderness before us. The foundational text for our journey is the work of Henri Tajfel and John Turner, who in the 1970s gifted the world Social Identity Theory. In essence, the theory posits a three-step process to our social lives: we categorize ourselves and others into groups, we identify with our chosen group, and then, in a move that will surprise no one who has ever been in a team-based game, we compare our group to others and conclude that ours is vastly superior. This is the psychological engine that drives everything from national pride to the unshakeable belief that your *World of Warcraft* guild, “The Dragon Slayers,” is an elite cadre of strategic masterminds, while the rival guild, “The Puppy Kickers,” is a disorganized rabble of mouth-breathing troglodytes.

This tendency towards in-group favoritism and out-group derogation is a well-documented phenomenon. It’s the reason we feel a surge of pride when a fellow guild member acquires a legendary item and a surge of schadenfreude when a rival guild wipes on a boss they’ve been attempting for weeks. It’s the “us vs. them” mentality writ large, or in this case, writ in the glowing pixels of a fantasy world. Academics, in their infinite wisdom, have even deigned to study this in the context of gaming. A 2015 study by Guegan, Moliner, and Buisine, for instance, found that guild membership is a powerful component of a player’s social identity, and that this identification is even more pronounced when players are physically in the game. This is the academic way of saying what every gamer already knows: your guild is your family, at least until you log off.

Further research by Kou and Gui in 2014 on “playing with strangers” highlights the double-edged sword of social interaction in games. While cooperation is often essential for success, it is also a fertile breeding ground for conflict. The study notes that unsolicited advice is often perceived as an insult, a finding that will resonate with anyone who has ever been told to “learn to play your class” by a self-proclaimed expert in a pick-up group. These studies, while valuable, often have the humorous quality of a scientist discovering that water is, in fact, wet. They confirm with rigorous methodology what gamers have been experiencing for decades: that online games are a petri dish for some of the most fascinating, and often petty, social dynamics imaginable. The literature provides a solid, if somewhat dry, foundation for our exploration. Now, let’s add the jokes.

**ANALYSIS**

At its core, the online game is a digital campfire, a luminous hearth around which we gather to share stories, forge alliances, and, most importantly, feel a sense of belonging. In a world that is increasingly fragmented and isolating, the allure of a tight-knit community is a powerful one. Guilds, clans, and corporations in online games are the modern-day tribes, offering a ready-made social structure complete with a chieftain (the guild leader), a council of elders (the officers), and a shared set of values and goals (the guild charter, which no one ever reads). This fulfills a deep-seated psychological need for community, a need that has been with us since we were huddled in caves, painting pictures of bison and trying to avoid being eaten by saber-toothed tigers.

But the journey from the broad, inclusive identity of “gamer” to the exclusive, narrow identity of a clique member is a fascinating one. It’s a process of social distillation, a gradual narrowing of the in-group. It begins with a broad allegiance, such as the iconic “For the Horde!” or “For the Alliance!” in *World of Warcraft*. This is a large, impersonal in-group, a way of dividing the entire game world into two manageable camps. But within these factions, a more meaningful identity is found in the guild. This is where the real social bonding occurs, where inside jokes are born, and where lifelong friendships are forged. Yet, even within the seemingly cozy confines of the guild, the process of social distillation continues. Small, exclusive cliques emerge, often based on skill level, shared interests, or simply the fact that a few players happen to be in the same time zone.

This is where the psychology of exclusion comes into play. While some cliques are formed for purely practical reasons—a group of highly skilled players who want to tackle the most challenging content, for instance—others are born of a more insidious impulse: elitism. The formation of an exclusive in-group is, by definition, an act of exclusion. It is a way of saying, “We are special, and you are not.” This can be a powerful motivator, both for those within the clique and for those on the outside looking in. For the insiders, it reinforces their sense of status and self-worth. For the outsiders, it can create a powerful desire to be accepted, to be part of the “in” crowd. This dynamic has a profound impact on consumer behavior within the game. Players will spend real money on in-game items to signal their status and their affiliation with a particular group. They will subscribe to a game long after they’ve grown bored of the content, simply to maintain their social connections. Game developers are keenly aware of this, and they design their games to encourage and monetize these in-group dynamics, with features like guild housing, customizable guild tabards, and exclusive group content.

**REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES**

Nowhere are the hilarious and terrifying consequences of in-group dynamics more apparent than in the lawless, digital frontier of *EVE Online*. This is a game where the “us vs. them” mentality is not just a social dynamic, but the very engine of the game itself. Corporations, the *EVE* equivalent of guilds, engage in espionage, sabotage, and all-out warfare on a scale that would make a Bond villain blush. The infamous “Bloodbath of B-R5RB,” a 21-hour battle that involved thousands of players and resulted in the virtual equivalent of trillions of dollars in damages, is a testament to the insane levels of in-group loyalty and out-group hatred that the game can inspire. The stories of betrayal in *EVE Online* are legendary, with players spending months, or even years, infiltrating a rival corporation, only to steal all their assets and leave them in ruins. It is the ultimate expression of the in-group/out-group dynamic, a digital Machiavellian playground where the only rule is that there are no rules.

If *EVE Online* is a dark, cynical satire of human nature, then *World of Warcraft* is a lighthearted sitcom. The guild drama in *WoW* is the stuff of internet legend, a rich tapestry of petty squabbles, romantic entanglements, and loot-related meltdowns. There’s the classic trope of the guild leader’s significant other, who is invariably a terrible player but is showered with the best gear. There’s the officer clique, a shadowy cabal that makes all the important decisions in a private Discord channel, leaving the rank-and-file members to wonder why the guild is suddenly switching its focus to underwater basket weaving. And, of course, there is the endless, soul-crushing drama over loot distribution, a process that can turn the most mild-mannered accountant into a raging, foam-mouthed berserker. These stories, endlessly recounted on Reddit and in guild forums, are a testament to the fact that even in a world of dragons and magic, our biggest problems are still other people.

This phenomenon is not limited to *EVE* and *WoW*. In *League of Legends*, the pre-made team of five friends who have been playing together for years is a formidable and often terrifying opponent for a group of five strangers thrown together by the matchmaking system. In *Destiny*, the quest to find a competent and non-toxic raid group is a game in itself. And in *Final Fantasy XIV*, the Free Companies, with their elaborate houses and social events, are a masterclass in creating a sense of belonging and in-group pride. The specific dynamics may vary from game to game, but the underlying principle remains the same: we are all just looking for our tribe.

**DISCUSSION**

So, what does this all mean? What can we learn from the fact that we spend our leisure time in fantastical digital worlds, only to recreate the same social hierarchies and divisions that plague us in the real world? The most obvious, and most humorous, conclusion is that we are, at our core, a bunch of tribalistic monkeys with opposable thumbs and a penchant for glowing loot. The technology may have changed, but the fundamental software of our social brains has not. We are hardwired to seek out like-minded individuals, to form groups, and to view those outside our group with a mixture of suspicion and contempt. It’s not a bug, it’s a feature.

This has profound implications for our understanding of consumer behavior. The desire to belong is one of the most powerful motivators of human action, and this is especially true in the context of online gaming. Game developers have become masters of leveraging these in-group dynamics to drive engagement and monetization. The sale of cosmetic items that signal guild affiliation, the creation of exclusive content that can only be accessed by organized groups, the very design of the subscription model, which encourages players to keep paying to stay with their friends—all of these are examples of how the psychology of in-group/out-group dynamics is used to shape consumer habits. We are not just paying for a game; we are paying for a community, a social identity, a place to belong.

Of course, there is a dark side to all of this. The same in-group loyalty that can lead to lifelong friendships can also foster toxicity, bullying, and the exclusion of new players. The clique that is so welcoming to its own members can be a hostile and impenetrable fortress to outsiders. The line between a healthy, supportive community and a toxic, elitist clique is a fine one, and it is a line that is all too easily crossed in the anonymous and often consequence-free environment of the internet. While it is easy to laugh at the absurdity of guild drama, it is also important to remember that behind every avatar is a real person, with real feelings. But let’s not get too serious, this is a humorous paper after all.

**CONCLUSION**

In the grand, pixelated tapestry of online gaming, the threads of in-group loyalty and out-group rivalry are woven so deeply that they are indistinguishable from the fabric of the game itself. We have seen that the formation of cliques is not a random or arbitrary process, but a predictable and deeply human one, driven by the ancient psychological imperatives of Social Identity Theory. From the corporate boardrooms of *EVE Online* to the raid dungeons of *World of Warcraft*, the story is the same: we seek community, we form groups, and then we spend an inordinate amount of time and energy defending the honor of our chosen tribe against all who would dare to challenge it.

In the end, the world of online gaming is a funhouse mirror, reflecting our own social tendencies back at us in a distorted and often hilarious way. It is a world where we can be heroes, villains, or just another face in the crowd. But no matter what role we choose to play, we can never truly escape the fundamental human need to belong, to be part of an “us.” And as long as there is an “us,” there will always be a “them.” So, the next time you find yourself on the outside of a digital clique, looking in, take heart. It’s not you, it’s them. And their guild tabard is ugly anyway.
references:
Guegan, J., Moliner, P., & Buisine, S. (2015). Why are online games so self-involving: A social identity analysis of massively multiplayer online role-playing games. European Journal of Social Psychology, 45(3), 349–355. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.2103
Kou, Y., & Gui, X. (2014). Playing with Strangers. University of California, Irvine.
Tajfel, H., & Turner, J. C. (1979). An integrative theory of intergroup conflict. In W. G. Austin & S. Worchel (Eds.), The social psychology of intergroup relations (pp. 33–47). Brooks/Cole.
